title: 2025/11/12 - EW
displayTitle: 2025-11-11 - Education and Workforce Committee - Petition of Dr Kerryn Palmer: Reinstate the Creatives in Schools Programme
topic: Petition of Dr Kerryn Palmer: Reinstate the Creatives in Schools Programme
description: Wednesday 12 November 2025 | Education and Workforce Committee Petition of Dr Kerryn Palmer: Reinstate the Creatives in Schools Programme Hearing of evidence: Dr Kerryn Palmer
descriptionSummary: Petition of Dr Kerryn Palmer: Reinstate the Creatives in Schools Programme. Dr Kerryn Palmer

chunks.text: Good morning. Good morning. Welcome, um, to the Education and Workforce Select Committee. Uh, my name's Carl b I'm the deputy chair of the committee, and, uh, great to welcome you this morning. We've got 15 minutes with you, including time for questions. Mm-hmm. Um, so I'll hand over to you and kick off, and then, uh, if you'll allow us some time to ask some questions as well, that'd Be great. Awesome. Thanks.

Kyle ua. Um, my name is Dr. Kirin Palmer. Thank you for taking the time to read and consider my petition and to hear more about how the arts are essential for health, wellbeing, education, and the development of young people, INO and New Zealand.

The importance of the arts for young people is grounded in international law. For example, article 31 of the UN Charter Convention and the rights of the child states that every child has the right to engage in play in recreational activities and to participate freely in cultural life in the arts. And it also says government should respect and promote the right of the child to participate fully in cultural and artistic life, and should encourage the provision of appropriate and equal opportunities for cultural, artistic,

recreational, and leisure activity. The importance of the arts for young people is grounded in international and national best practice pedagogy. Well, docu documented international and national studies demonstrate that children who engage in the arts typically achieve higher in literacy and numeracy and have greater levers of critical thinking, creativity, empathy, and resilience.

Notably, this has already been recognized by the central government through the recent arts Amplify strategy. Minister Goldsmith wants to nurture talent and encourage more New Zealanders to actively engage with New Zealand arts, culture and heritage. He has a vision that the next generation of Kiwis will grow up with access to a wide variety of arts and culture.

Unfortunately, he has committed today no resources to implement this ambitious strategy, and there seems to be no actual plan on how to achieve it. As an arts educator of 35 years, I, along with the support of hundreds of other New Zealanders who signed my petition, do have a plan.

A reinstatement and strengthening of the creatives and Schools program would provide economic, cultural and wellbeing outcomes to many ensure that all three of the arts amplify strategic outcomes are achieved. To summarize what you've already read, the Creatives and Schools program was an arts program that ran from 2019 to 2024.

It funded schools to partner with professional artists and creative practitioners to share specialist artistic knowledge and creative practice. It enabled children throughout ROA to participate in high quality arts rich experiences in their schools in collaboration with professional artists. This program empowered students, offered professional development for teachers and provided essential opportunities for hundreds of artists who are often amongst the lowest paid in our society.

It was truly a win-win for all involved. It was also also well documented, low cost, and proved to be very effective. A summarizing comment on the 2023 employment. Um, in 2023 report Read Creatives in Schools is a program that supports Akon and student learning, wellbeing, attendance and engagement.

Thousands of young people, teachers and artists benefited from the Creatives in Schools initiative before it was unexpected unexpectedly and brutally cut. In 2024, when I questioned Erica Sanford and Paul Goldsmith about why they had cut this program, Paul Goldsmith replied, the government recognizes the need for a more strategic and systematic approach to arts education.

And that Creatives and Schools was the successful program, but it only targeted a select number of schools. Well, now it's targeting none. And I am yet to see any evidence of a strategic and systematic approach to arts education from this current government.

In fact, I would argue that recently the opposite has been proposed. So as a result of the seemingly arbitrary decision, thousands of young people have missed out on quality arts experiences in the past 18 months. Students such as those from Wellington Girls who spoke to me about how a creatives in schools dance project enabled them to build the courage and autonomy to start their own dance practice and lead the next generation to create and perform.

The head of Dance and drama at this school said that as a result of the creatives and schools project, students engagement with school had skyrocketed. It allowed students to engage in critical thinking and took the burden of small departments in which teaching staff have no capacity to lead artistic projects.

These incredible pieces of art done by a 5-year-old, uh, Eloise. Oh, wow. And this is done by Bel, who's nine. These were part of a creatives and schools program at Newtown School. They employed visionary artists, Paul Forest, to work with the children.

Over several years, not only did these children learn about art, but they learned about maths and science and their own identity through the medium of art. The principal of Newtown School, Nikki Reed, called the Creatives and Schools Program, a connector, a program of partnership that connects artists and schools to create lasting legacies.

She said, we have learners who know they are artists. The program has sowing seeds of identity, fostered personal expression, and given our learners a willingness to try with an expert artist in our community, we have seen an increase in a passion for the arts and a growth in wellbeing. And to reassure you that there is also economic benefit to such a program, Java Dance Theater, the artistic director who is here today, for example, worked with 105 year olds at Cash Bear Avenue School to research tantrums.

The show that came from this research has now been performed to thousands of young New Zealanders from all over Altara, from central Otago to Auckland, and in 2027 has been bought by a, by the Big Dreams, um, festival in Australia, one of the most established festivals for young people in the world.

So this project not only nurtured young New Zealanders, but it's also become an export proving that the money spent in the Creatives and schools program has multiple impacts. These are just a small sample of the testimonies and examples I have received by researching the Creatives in Schools program.

You might be thinking or need reminding why do we even need the arts? What's the deal? Um, well, I can assure you that the deal is that the arts help people, young people engage their imaginations, dream big and imagine a better future. They encourage children to work collaboratively, solve problems, develop resilience, empathy, and think outside the square.

Studying through the arts is a proven way to strengthen life skills such as communication, leadership, critical thinking, work ethic, and time management. And considering the government's concern with student outcomes and results in numeracy and literacy, the Creatives and Schools program actively supported worldwide research that proves an arts rich education increases student achievement in all areas of the curriculum.

Evaluated benefits of the program also included enhanced wellbeing, re-engaging students with school, raising the confidence of teachers, developing more sustainable portfolio careers for artists and building better homeschool relationships with whanau and parents. The current government says that they are worried about school attendance, school achievement, and mental health amongst young people, the Creatives and Schools program provided solutions to many of these issues.

In the year 2000, New Zealand was near the top of the world with its pieces scores. One of the reasons I believe is because of our arts rich education and teacher training system that had been initiated in the 1950s by Clarence Bebe. Internationally, we only have to look at some of the top scoring piece of scoring countries such as Finland to realize that having arts as an integral part of education is a way to increase student achievement across the board.

The UK has recently realized this by implementing a new curriculum that re that integrates the arts. And I have recently given presentations to teachers in China who have realized that years of structured numeracy and literacy and literacy and testing have left many of their young people unequipped for the modern world.

They are now turning to the arts and creativity and putting it back into their curriculum. I fear New Zealand is not reading the room, or at least it's not connecting the dots. We once had a world leading education system. Other countries are recognizing the importance of the arts at the heart of the education system, and yet this government seems to be systematically narrowing the curriculum and ignoring worldwide trends and evidence that shows that this is the opposite of what this generation of young people needs.

A tried and tested solution to ensuring all children in New Zealand, not just those who can afford it, can have access to the arts, is by reinstating and strengthening the Creatives and schools program across all schools in New Zealand. Imagine that it would be a game changer. Not only would we see young people want to go to school and increase their achievement in all levels of the cur all areas of the curriculum, but we'd also see teachers feel supported and gain important professional development.

We would see communities connected with schools. We would see mental health and attendance rates improve. We would see numeracy and literacy rates improve. We would, I suspect, have a damn good shot of raising out peace of scores. I believe that when as a society, we undervalue the arts in favor of the economy and implement outdated educational methodology that focuses on individual student targets.

We are at risk of building a society that focuses on wealth stages and individual success rather than collective wellbeing, collaboration, and community. The young people of New Zealand deserve better. And as outlined earlier, a program such as creatives and schools focuses on collective wellbeing, collaboration, and community, as well as delivering benefits to the economy.

I call on the current government to reinstate and strengthen the creatives and schools program immediately, and by this I don't mean faff around for another year, roll out our lower cost better version of it. There is actually no need to reinvent the wheel. The program worked incredibly well. CRS and Schools was a low cost multi-agency funded program that worked for children, teachers, communities, and artists.

We believe that it needs to be reinstated and strengthened now so that all New Zealand children can benefit from it. Thank you. Well done. Thanks very much. Lawrence Zan. Thank you Chair. Um, thank you so much for that presentation. I think that was confirmed also in the independent review in 23 around the creatives in schools. And I think particularly it says at times the project were the only reason Ong students kept coming to school.

Mm-hmm. But I, so in terms of removing it and also the understanding of art as a subject overall. Mm-hmm. What do you think the government or parliament has gotten wrong about the perception of art? Uh, I think there's a huge, um, misunderstanding of the, the, the power of art and the value of art being integrated in the cu into the curriculum.

Okay. I think the, the, the moment with the, the new proposed arts curriculum, it's all being stripped back again. And we are, they're not understanding that actually having a foundation of the arts within education increases all of the things that they want to do. All of the wellbeing, all of the literacy, all of the numeracy.

And that by using something like the Krebs in Schools program, um, children want to come to school, they want to come to school, they want to be there, they want to be engaged with school, and they want to learn. Um, so yeah, I think they're missing a huge opportunity by this narrowing, um, of, of the, um, curriculum, but also the, um, you know, just really mandating that we do an hour a day of maths, an hour a day of reading, an hour a day of this.

It's kind of like this, we've done better than this in the past. We've had this integrated into our system and we've had better results from it. I think children are not loving going to school when it's really, when it, they're forced to do reading and maths and things like that. I think this is a way of encouraging them to do those things in a fun way and in a more productive way.

Mm-hmm. Vanessa, we Thank you much. Um, I haven't, um, read too much into this previously about the, um, funding, um, allocation. Do you know what the total allocation of funding was per annum? Um, I'm not sure it, but it was what I do know that it was from Creative New Zealand to the Ministry of Education and the, um, ministry of Culture and Heritage.

And I think the last, the last one was, was only like 3000, uh, three, sorry, $3 million was I think the last 20, 23 I think. I can't remember. But yeah, it was only, it was, it was quite a small amount. So, Um, and, and if we talk about the, the extent of coverage, um, what percentage of schools were covered with that 3 million?

There were, I think there were 250 Pro, we're just talking about the last year that they did, I mean 250 programs, so 250 schools. Okay. Thanks Rachel. Um, thanks chair. Thanks. Um, uh, Dr. Palmer for an excellent submission. You talked about the, um, uh, strategy amplify.

So I'm just keen to understand, uh, in that strategy it does talk about the importance of education. Are you seeing a disconnect between what the strategy's saying and what government's actually rolling out in terms of implementation? Yeah, I'm absolutely, and it talks a lot about pathways and it talks about the importance of, of all children and all young people having access to the arts, but then there's no, there's no plan for that.

There's no, like, how are they going to have access to the arts? Um, for me, a big concern is that if, if arts are not integrated into schools, the children that can't afford to do after school ballet classes and theater classes just don't have access and talking to people in the regions, they don't have the chance to take people to the theater, for example, because they live in Invercargill for, for example.

So actually by having it in school, then those strategic outcomes within the arts amplify strategy can be realized because then every child can have access to the arts. Okay. Thank you very much. Uh, that's all we've got time for this morning. I appreciate you taking time to come and Thank you so much.
Um, members, we moving.
